name: Prydwen
description: Prydwen is the name of King Arthur's ship in Welsh Arthurian tradition. Its name translates roughly to 'Fair Form' or 'Blessed Form'. It appears primarily in the poem 'Preiddeu Annwfn' ('The Spoils of Annwn') found in the Book of Taliesin. In this poem, Arthur leads an expedition to Annwn, the Welsh Otherworld, to retrieve a magical cauldron. The poem describes the perilous journey and the small number of men who returned, suggesting the Prydwen was instrumental in navigating the treacherous waters and dangers of Annwn. While the poem doesn't explicitly detail the ship's powers, the fact that it could traverse the Otherworld unharmed implies a degree of magical protection or inherent seaworthiness beyond that of ordinary vessels. The Prydwen is therefore seen as a symbol of Arthur's power and his ability to venture into realms beyond mortal comprehension, and a testament to the enduring allure and mystery of the Welsh Otherworld. Its significance lies in its connection to a pivotal Arthurian quest and its role as a vessel capable of bridging the gap between the mortal and supernatural realms. The Prydwen's journey to Annwn is often interpreted as a metaphor for spiritual quests and the challenges of facing the unknown. It is also associated with the themes of bravery, leadership, and the pursuit of knowledge.
subtitle: Arthur's Ship of Annwn
material: Unspecified, likely wood with magical enhancements
origin: Presumably crafted for or gifted to King Arthur
currentLocation: Unknown, likely lost or decayed over time

keyMyths.title: The Spoils of Annwn
keyMyths.description: Arthur sails Prydwen to Annwn to retrieve a magical cauldron. Only seven men return from the perilous journey.

keyMyths.title: Voyage to the Otherworld
keyMyths.description: Prydwen's journey highlights Arthur's ability to traverse between worlds. It symbolizes his power and connection to the supernatural.

sources.author: Taliesin
sources.description: Poem detailing Arthur's voyage to Annwn on Prydwen
